---
title: "Houston Annual Temperature Trends"
author: "Oluchi Nnadi"
date: "2026-06-23"
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    code-fold: show
    embed-resources: true
---

## Yearly Average Temperature Analysis

This analysis tracks how Houston's average annual high temperature has evolved over 30 years (1996-2025), revealing long-term climate trends in the region.

```{r}
#| label: yearly-average
#| warning: false
#| message: false
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6
#| echo: false

library(jsonlite)
library(dplyr)
library(lubridate)
library(ggplot2)

# 1. Load Data
json_file <- "houston_temp.json"
if (file.exists(json_file)) {
  raw_data <- fromJSON(json_file)
} else {
  url <- "http://archive-api.open-meteo.com/v1/archive?latitude=29.7633&longitude=-95.3633&start_date=1996-01-01&end_date=2025-12-31&daily=temperature_2m_max&temperature_unit=fahrenheit&timezone=America%2FChicago"
  raw_data <- fromJSON(url)
}

# 2. Convert to data frame and calculate yearly averages
yearly_avg <- raw_data$daily |> 
  as.data.frame() |> 
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(time),
    year = year(date),
    temp = temperature_2m_max
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(temp)) |>
  group_by(year) |>
  summarise(
    avg_temp = mean(temp),
    min_temp = min(temp),
    max_temp = max(temp),
    .groups = 'drop'
  )

# 3. Calculate trend line
trend_model <- lm(avg_temp ~ year, data = yearly_avg)
trend_data <- data.frame(
  year = yearly_avg$year,
  trend = predict(trend_model)
)

# 4. Create the plot
ggplot(yearly_avg, aes(x = year, y = avg_temp)) +
  # Main line connecting yearly averages
  geom_line(color = "#ffb74d", linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.8) +
  
  # Points for each year
  geom_point(aes(fill = avg_temp), color = "#ff9800", size = 4, shape = 21, stroke = 1.5) +
  
  # Color gradient for points (cooler to warmer)
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#4a90e2",
    high = "#ff6f00",
    name = "Avg Temp (°F)"
  ) +
  
  # Linear trend line showing overall direction
  geom_line(data = trend_data, aes(y = trend), 
            color = "#ff6f00", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.7) +
  
  # Annotations for trend
  annotate("text", x = 1998, y = max(yearly_avg$avg_temp) - 0.5,
           label = sprintf("Trend: +%.2f°F per decade", 
                          coef(trend_model)[2] * 10),
           size = 3.5, color = "#ff6f00", fontface = "bold", hjust = 0) +
  
  # Scales and labels
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks = seq(1996, 2025, 5),
    labels = seq(1996, 2025, 5)
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(73, 80),
    breaks = seq(73, 80, 1),
    labels = paste0(seq(73, 80, 1), "°F")
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Houston Average Annual High Temperature Trend (1996-2025)",
    subtitle = "30-Year Analysis: How Houston's Climate Is Changing",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Average Annual High Temperature (°F)",
    caption = "Data Source: Open-Meteo Historical Weather API\nTrendline shows overall warming pattern over the 30-year period."
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal(base_family = "sans") +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 15, color = "#2c3e50", margin = margin(b = 6)),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "#7f8c8d", margin = margin(b = 15)),
    axis.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 11, color = "#34495e"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10, color = "#2c3e50"),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_line(color = "#ecf0f1", linewidth = 0.5),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "#ecf0f1", linewidth = 0.5),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 9),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 8, color = "#bdc3c7", hjust = 0, margin = margin(t = 15))
  )
```

## Key Observations

The yearly average temperature analysis reveals important climate patterns:

- **Overall Warming Trend**: The dashed orange line shows a clear upward trajectory, indicating Houston has experienced warming over the past three decades
- **Year-to-Year Variability**: Despite the overall trend, individual years show natural fluctuations in average temperatures
- **Color Gradient**: Cooler years appear in blue, while warmer years shift toward red/orange, providing a visual representation of temperature changes
- **Decade Perspective**: The trend line quantifies the rate of change, typically measured in degrees per decade

This data supports broader climate science findings about temperature increases in urban areas and the Gulf Coast region.
